selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: You know, I have to be honest with you—I've never actually been on social media myself, and honestly, that decision has served me well, especially when I see what my children and their friends deal with every day. But I understand the problem is real, and I've picked up enough from conversations with my clients at the gym and from my family to have some thoughts on this. The fitness industry, in particular, has become a minefield of fake transformations and impossible promises. I see people come to me devastated because they believed some influencer's before-and-after photos that were clearly doctored or achieved through unhealthy means. What I've learned from my own experience as a trainer is that authenticity matters, and the same applies to navigating these digital spaces. If I were to advise someone, I'd say first, develop a healthy skepticism. Don't accept everything at face value. When you see dramatic claims about weight loss or muscle gain, ask yourself if it's realistic. In my profession, I know that real, sustainable change takes time and consistency—there are no shortcuts, despite what the internet wants you to believe. Second, seek out sources that show the human behind the content. Real people have bad angles, inconsistent lighting, and days when they don't look their best. Third, if something makes you feel inadequate or anxious, that's your signal to step back. Given my own struggles with depression, I understand how vulnerable we become when we're not feeling our best, and fake content preys on that vulnerability. Finally, talk to people you trust—your family, your doctor, your trainer—before making decisions based on what you've seen online. The world is complicated enough without adding layers of manufactured reality on top of it.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona is a 60-year-old self-employed fitness trainer from Bari with no social media presence, high neuroticism (89), low extraversion and agreeableness, moderate openness, and low conscientiousness. Her background in fitness and personal experience with depression inform her perspective. Her lack of social media engagement means she approaches the topic from an outside observer's viewpoint, informed by professional experience and family conversations. Her high neuroticism suggests anxiety about the topic, while her moderate openness allows for understanding different perspectives. Her vocational background means she speaks from practical, experiential knowledge rather than academic theory. The Hindu beliefs and Italian cultural context add subtle dimensions to her worldview about authenticity and community trust.
response_parsed.tone: cautious, experienced, practical, somewhat protective, tinged with anxiety but grounded in real-world wisdom from professional practice and family observation
citations: personal professional experience as fitness trainer
